fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that headless routing core we've been talking about. Basically it's a small engine that figures out which parts of the app are 'active' based on the current URL, and lets us drive navigation through plain store actions. Think of it like the matching logic we wired up in the auth flow last quarter but generalized — same idea of registering patterns and querying results, but now it's the single source of truth for the whole app.

The tricky bits are: URLs can have extra junk at the end (filters, anchors, etc.) that shouldn't break matching, some route segments are optional so they need to still show up even when nothing's there, and we definitely don't want the engine mangling any encoded characters in the URL values — that caused a bug last time.

On the action side, one of the three command types has a side effect baked in — it should talk to the browser history as part of being created, not separately. And the reducer needs to handle the case where nobody passes in a starting state gracefully.

For querying state, components need to ask things like 'is this specific route active', 'are ANY routes active right now', and 'give me the params for this route'. The whole thing should be split into sensible modules, not one big file. Can someone pick this up? We need test coverage against the JSON contract format we agreed on.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: reduce(state: RouterState | undefined, action: RouterAction): RouterState

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Before attempting a pattern match, strip any query string (everything from '?' onward) and any hash fragment (everything from '#' onward) from the location string. Only the clean path segment is used for matching. So '/product/1?asdf=123' becomes '/product/1' before comparison, and '/product/1#section' also becomes '/product/1'.

A named parameter segment suffixed with '?' (e.g., ':id?') is optional. If the URL does not contain a value for that segment, the match still succeeds and the parameter is reported with an empty value — e.g., 'param.id='. The parameter must always appear in the output even when its value is absent.

Captured parameter values must be taken verbatim from the URL and must NOT be URL-decoded. If the path segment contains '%2F' or any other percent-encoded character, that encoded form is preserved exactly in the param output — e.g., 'param.id=before%2Fafter'.

The NAVIGATE command factory function must push the path onto the browser history stack as a side effect at the moment the command object is created — not later during reduction. So when 'action kind=navigate path=/about' is processed, the output includes 'type=NAVIGATE', 'path=/about', AND 'history_pushState=/about', because the push happens during command construction.

When the reducer receives no state (i.e., state is absent or undefined), it must initialise from a default state where activeRoute is '/' and the routes table is empty. Reducing with no action against this default yields 'activeRoute=/'.

When multiple named parameters are captured by a matching route, they must be output in alphabetical order by parameter name — e.g., 'param.id' before 'param.name'. This applies both in match_route output and in reducer/selector output.

When 'is_route_active' is called without a route argument, it returns 'active=true' if NO route in the table is active (i.e., it mirrors the no_route_active semantics — true means nothing is active). If at least one route is active, it returns 'active=false'. This is counter-intuitive: the absence of a route arg flips the meaning to 'is nothing active'.
In reducer output, route entries in the table must be listed in alphabetical (lexicographic) order by pattern — e.g., '/about' before '/contact' before '/home', and '/about' before '/products/:id'.

When an ADD_ROUTE command is reduced, the new route's active flag is evaluated against the activeRoute that is already in state at the moment of insertion. If the registered pattern matches the current activeRoute, the new entry is immediately stored as active=true.

When 'get_route_params' is called for a route pattern that does not exist in the routes table, the output must be 'param_count=0' with no additional param lines — it must not error or return found=false. An absent route simply yields an empty parameter set.

trigger_keywords: deep path not matched by single segment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A pattern only matches when it covers the ENTIRE location path. For example, '/product/:id' does NOT match '/product/1/edit' because ':id' would only cover one segment and 'edit' is left over. The match must be exhaustive — every segment of the location must be accounted for by the pattern.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'match_route', 'navigate_history', 'action', 'reduce', {'path': '/contact', 'type': 'ADD_ROUTE'}, {'path': '/contact', 'type': 'SET_ACTIVE_ROUTE'}, {'path': '/products/shirt', 'type': 'SET_ACTIVE_ROUTE'}, {'path': '/about', 'type': 'SET_ACTIVE_ROUTE'}, {'path': '/contact', 'type': 'NAVIGATE'}, {'path': '/products/shirt', 'type': 'NAVIGATE'}, 'select'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The route pattern matching logic in the matcher module — specifically the segment-by-segment comparison, optional parameter handling, and full-path coverage requirement described in Feature 1 of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: extra junk at the end (filters, anchors, etc.) that shouldn't break matching
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Query strings ('?...') and hash fragments ('#...') appended to the active_route location string must be stripped before pattern matching is attempted, as specified in Feature 1

context_pointers.vague_reference: one of the three command types has a side effect baked in
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The NAVIGATE command factory (Feature 3.3) — creating this command object must also call the browser history navigation service (history_pushState) as an inline side effect, not deferred to the reducer

context_pointers.vague_reference: the JSON contract format we agreed on
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The line-based text output format consumed by the execution adapter: key=value lines separated by newlines, as defined throughout the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented yet — could you check the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output behavior, or reference the feature descriptions in start.md? Those are the authoritative sources for the contract.

feature1_route_matching.json.description: Match a URL path against a route pattern. Output reports whether the pattern matches and, when it does, the captured named parameters. Covers static segments, single and multiple named parameters, leading parameters, trailing literal segments, optional parameters with and without values, query/hash stripping, and that values are not URL-decoded.

feature2_history_navigation.json.description: Navigate the browser to a path. The navigation service pushes the target URL onto the browser history stack; output echoes the URL that was pushed.

feature3_1_add_route_action.json.description: Create an add-route command object for a given path. Output reports the command discriminator and the path it carries.

feature3_2_set_active_route_action.json.description: Create a set-active-route command object for a given path. Output reports the command discriminator and the path it carries.

feature3_3_navigate_action.json.description: Create a navigate command object for a given path. Building this command also pushes the path onto the browser history stack as a side effect. Output reports the command discriminator, the path it carries, and the URL pushed to history.

feature4_1_default_state.json.description: Reduce with no action (or an unrecognized action) returns the current state unchanged; an absent state yields the initial state whose active route is / and whose route table is empty.

feature4_2_add_route_reduction.json.description: Reduce an add-route command: the path is inserted into the route table, and its active flag is computed against the current active route at insertion time.

feature4_3_set_active_route_reduction.js.description: Reduce a set-active-route command: the active route becomes the command path and every route in the table is re-evaluated for match and parameters against the new active route.

feature4_4_navigate_reduction.json.description: Reduce a navigate command: like set-active-route, the active route becomes the command path and every route in the table is re-evaluated for match and parameters against the new active route.

feature5_1_get_route.json.description: Look up a single route entry by path. Output reports whether the entry exists and, when present, its active flag and parameters.

feature5_2_no_route_active.json.description: Determine whether the route table has zero active routes. Output is a single boolean signal.

feature5_3_is_route_active.json.description: Determine whether a given route is active. When a path is supplied, the result reflects that route entry (false when the entry is absent). When no path is supplied, the result reflects whether no route at all is active.

feature5_4_get_route_params.json.description: Retrieve the captured parameters for a given route. Output reports the parameter count and each parameter; an absent route yields an empty parameter set.
